perturbation.original_text: The recipient agrees to hold all information provided by Manitoba Housing (“Confidential Information”) in trust and confidence and agrees that it shall be used only for the contemplated purposes, shall not be used for any other purpose or disclosed to any third part.
perturbation.changed_text: The recipient agrees to hold all information provided by Manitoba Housing (“Confidential Information”) with reasonable care and agrees that it should be used primarily for the contemplated purposes, and shall not be intentionally disclosed to unrelated third parties.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'reasonable care' is ambiguous and weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. 'Primarily for the contemplated purposes' introduces uncertainty. 'Intentionally disclosed to unrelated third parties' leaves room for interpretation on what constitutes intentional and unrelated, creating a loophole for potential breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Personal Information Protection and Identity Theft Prevention Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Principle 4.7 requires personal information to be protected by security safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information. The modified text, using 'reasonable care' instead of a strict obligation, may not meet the required security standards for protecting confidential information under PIPEDA, especially considering the data is being held 'in trust' on behalf of Manitoba Housing. The changes create ambiguity where PIPEDA demands demonstrable diligence.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Privacy Act Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" Skip to section menu Exemptions (continued) Responsibilities of Government (continued) Marginal note: Federal-provincial affairs 20 Â The head of a government institution may refuse to disclose any personal information requested under subsection 12(1) the disclosure of which could reasonably be expected to be injurious to the conduct by the Government of Canada of federal-provincial affairs. 1980-81-82-83, c. 111, Sch. II â20â Marginal note: International affairs and defence 21 Â The head of a government institution may refuse to disclose any personal information requested under subsection 12(1) the disclosure of which could reasonably be expected to be injurious to the conduct of international affairs, the defence of Canada or any state allied or associated with Canada, as defined in subsection 15(2) of the Access to Information Act , or the efforts of Canada toward detecting, preventing or suppressing subversive or hostile activities, as defined in subsection 15(2) of the Access to Information Act , including, without restricting the generality of the foregoing, any such information listed in paragraphs 15(1)(a) to (i) of the Access to Information Act . 1980-81-82-83, c. 111, Sch. II â21â Marginal note: Law enforcement and investigation 22 Â (1) Â The head of a government institution may refuse to disclose any personal information requested under subsection 12(1) (a) Â that was obtained or prepared by any government institution, or part of any government institution, that is an investigative body specified in the regulations in the course of lawful investigations pertaining to (i) Â the detection, prevention or suppression of crime, (ii) Â the enforcement of any law of Canada or a province, or (iii) Â activities suspected of constituting threats to the security of Canada within the meaning of the Canadian Security Intelligence Service Act , if the information came into existence less than twenty years prior to the request; (b) Â the disclosure of which could reasonably be expected to be injurious to the enforcement of any law of Canada or a province or the conduct of lawful investigations, including, without restricting the generality of the foregoing, any such information (i) Â relating to the existence or nature of a particular investigation, (ii) Â that would reveal the identity of a confidential source of information, or (iii) Â that was obtained or prepared in the course of an investigation; or (c) Â the disclosure of which could reasonably be expected to be injurious to the security of penal institutions. Marginal note: Policing services for provinces or municipalities (2) Â The head of a government institution shall refuse to disclose any personal information requested under subsection 12(1) that was obtained or prepared by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police while performing policing services for a province or municipality pursuant to an arrangement made under section 20 of the Royal Canadian Mounted Police Act , where the Government of Canada has, on the request of the province or municipality, agreed not to disclose such information. Marginal note: Definition of investigation (3) Â For the purposes of paragraph (1)(b), investigation means an investigation that (a) Â pertains to the administration or enforcement of an Act of Parliament; (b) Â is authorized by or pursuant to an Act of Parliament; or (c) Â is within a class of investigations specified in the regulations. 1980-81-82-83, c. 111, Sch. II â22â 1984, c. 21, s. 90, c. 40, s. 79(E) Marginal note: Information obtained by Privacy Commissioner 22.1 Â (1) Â The Privacy Commissioner shall refuse to disclose any personal information requested under this Act that was obtained or created by the Commissioner or on the Commissionerâs behalf in the course of an investigation conducted by, or under the authority of, the Commissioner or that was obtained by the Commissioner in the course of a consulta
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the protection of confidential information. The original text states that confidential information "shall not be used for any other purpose or disclosed to any third part," creating a strict obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating the information "should be used primarily for the contemplated purposes" and "shall not be intentionally disclosed to unrelated third parties." This creates loopholes. The LLM's law explanation points out how PIPEDA Principle 4.7 requires security safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information. The modified text, using 'reasonable care' instead of a strict obligation, may not meet the required security standards. The change from "shall not" to "should primarily" and the addition of "intentionally disclosed to unrelated third parties" weakens the obligation to a degree that it could create a legal contradiction. While the provided scraped snippets don't directly address PIPEDA, the principle of protecting sensitive information is undermined by the ambiguities introduced.

perturbation.original_text: At the conclusion of any discussions or upon demand by Manitoba Housing, a ll confidential information or written notes taken shall be returned to Manitoba Housing.
perturbation.changed_text: At the reasonable conclusion of discussions or upon written request by Manitoba Housing, most confidential information or relevant written notes should be returned to Manitoba Housing when practical.
perturbation.explanation: 'Reasonable conclusion' introduces subjectivity about when discussions end. 'Written request' adds a condition. 'Most confidential information' and 'relevant written notes' create ambiguity regarding what must be returned. 'When practical' provides an escape clause, weakening the obligation to return all materials.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Personal Information Protection and Identity Theft Prevention Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Principle 4.5 requires the organization to destroy, erase, or make anonymous personal information that is no longer required to fulfill the identified purposes. By introducing 'most confidential information' and 'relevant written notes' and 'when practical', the modification leaves room for not returning all confidential information and written notes. As such, this could result in a failure to comply with PIPEDA Principle 4.5.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities and escape clauses that weaken the original obligation to return all confidential information, potentially leading to non-compliance with data protection principles as explained in the Law Explanation. The original contract mandated the return of 'all' confidential information, whereas the modified version introduces 'most' and 'relevant,' creating room for interpretation and potential non-compliance. Also, the addition of "when practical" provides an escape clause.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential information shall not be disclosed to any employee, consultant or third party unless they have executed and agreed to be bound by the terms of the Agreement, and have been approved by Manitoba Housing.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential information should generally not be disclosed to employees, consultants, or third parties unless reasonable efforts have been made to ensure they are aware of the terms of the Agreement, and have been considered acceptable by Manitoba Housing.
perturbation.explanation: 'Should generally not' weakens the prohibition. 'Reasonable efforts have been made to ensure they are aware' is vague. 'Considered acceptable' is subjective, differing from 'approved', creating a lower threshold for disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Personal Information Protection and Identity Theft Prevention Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Principle 4.1 states that an organization is responsible for personal information in its possession or control and shall designate an individual or individuals who are accountable for the organization's compliance with the principles. By reducing the required diligence with 'reasonable efforts have been made' and weakening the approval with 'considered acceptable', the change may prevent proper accountability for personal information. As such, this might impede adherence to PIPEDA, Principle 4.1

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that confidential information is not to be disclosed unless certain conditions are met, creating a clear obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation by using 'should generally not' and 'reasonable efforts,' introducing ambiguity and lowering the standard for disclosure. The Law Explanation indicates this impedes adherence to PIPEDA, Principle 4.1 regarding accountability for personal information. While the scraped snippets do not directly address this specific obligation, the explanation's reference to accountability and the weakening of the original obligation strongly suggest a contradiction. The modified text creates a legal loophole that could undermine the protection of confidential information and the accountability required under privacy principles.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and its validity, construction and effect shall be governed by the laws of Manitoba.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and its validity, construction and effect shall be generally interpreted in accordance with the prevailing legal principles of Manitoba.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'governed by the laws' with 'generally interpreted in accordance with the prevailing legal principles' introduces uncertainty. 'Prevailing legal principles' is vague and allows for subjective interpretation, rather than strict adherence to Manitoba law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 8 of the Manitoba Interpretation Act mandates that every enactment must be construed to give effect to its provisions. The modified text, by stating the agreement should be 'generally interpreted' creates ambiguity that undermines this mandatory construction, potentially leading to interpretations that deviate from the intended effect of Manitoba laws relevant to the agreement.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Public Involvement in Health Act 2007 Offender Management Act 2007 UK Borders Act 2007 European Union (Amendment) Act 2008 Housing and Regeneration Act 2008 Budget Responsibility and National Audit Act 2011 Police Reform and Social Responsibility Act 2011 Charities Act 2011 Crime and Courts Act 2013 Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 Infrastructure Act 2015 Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018 Legislation (Wales) Act 2019 European Union (Withdrawal Agreement) Act 2020 Fisheries Act 2020 Sentencing Act 2020 Judicial Review and Courts Act 2022 Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Act 2023 Status: Amended Text of statute as originally enacted Revised text of statute as amended Text of the Interpretation Act 1978 as in force today (including any amendments) within the United Kingdom, from legislation.gov.uk . The Interpretation Act 1978 [ a ] (c. 30) is an act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom . The act makes provision for the interpretation of acts of Parliament, [ 1 ] Measures of the General Synod of the Church of England , Measures of the Church Assembly , [ 2 ] subordinate legislation , [ 3 ] "deeds and other instruments and documents", [ 4 ] acts of the Scottish Parliament and instruments made thereunder (added 1998), [ 5 ] and Measures and acts of the National Assembly for Wales and instruments made thereunder. [ 6 ] The act makes provision in relation to: the construction of certain words and phrases, words of enactment , amendment or repeal of Acts in the Session they were passed, judicial notice , commencement, statutory powers and duties, the effect of repeals, and duplicated offences. The Interpretation Act (Northern Ireland) 1954 [ 7 ] applies in the same way to Acts of the Parliament of Northern Ireland or Acts of the Northern Ireland Assembly . Section 7 [ edit ] Section 7 of the act, concerned with service of documents by post, replaced section 26 of the Interpretation Act 1889 ( 52 & 53 Vict. c. 63). [ 8 ] It states that Where an Act authorises or requires any document to be served by post (whether the expression "serve" or the expression "give" or "send" or any other expression is used) then, unless the contrary intention appears, the service is deemed to be effected by properly addressing, pre-paying and posting a letter containing the document and, unless the contrary is proved, to have been effected at the time at which the letter would be delivered in the ordinary course of post. [ 9 ] Interpretation of the words unless the contrary is proved is discussed in the case of Calladine-Smith v Saveorder Ltd , as to whether the "contrary" means the contrary of the allegation that the letter was properly addressed, prepaid and posted, or whether it refers to the contrary of the deeming provision that the letter in question was delivered in the ordinary course of post. In this particular case, a letter was shown to have been properly addressed, pre-paid and posted, but not received. On a balance of probabilities the court accepted that the "contrary" to the deemed provision had been proved, the letter was not received, and therefore the deemed provision could not hold. [ 10 ] Section 16 – General savings [ edit ] The following cases are relevant to this section: Hough v Windus (1884) 12 QBD 224, CA R v Fisher (Charles) [1969] 1 WLR 8, CA R v West London Stipendiary Magistrate, ex parte Simeon [1983] AC 234, HL Section 18 – Duplicated offences [ edit ] Section 18 of the act provides: Where an act or omission constitutes an offence under two or more Acts, or both under an Act and at common law, the offender shall, unless the contrary intention appears, be liable to be prosecuted and punished under either or any of those Acts or at common law, but shall not be liable to be punished more than once for the same offence. [ 11 ] This section replaces section 33 of the Interpretation Act 1889 ( 52 & 53 Vict. c. 63). [ 12 ] Humphreys J. said that that section did not add anythi
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating the agreement should be 'generally interpreted in accordance with the prevailing legal principles' instead of being 'governed by the laws' of Manitoba. This weakens the mandatory application of Manitoba law, as highlighted by the LLM explanation referencing Section 8 of the Manitoba Interpretation Act which mandates construing enactments to give effect to their provisions. The modified text allows for subjective interpretation which could deviate from the intended effect of Manitoba laws, creating a legal ambiguity.
